corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Although the trained model achieves good validation accuracy, it does not generalize well (82% test accuracy).
Future work includes explaining decisions made by deep networks in domains such as reinforcement learning, natural language processing and video applications.
We introduce a new way of combining feature maps using the gradient signal that does not require any modification in the network architecture.
We introduce a new way of combining feature maps using the gradient signal that does not require any modification in the network architecture.

In recent work, Zhang et al. introduce contrastive Marginal Winning Probability (c-MWP), a probabilistic Winner-Take-All formulation for modelling the top-down attention for neural classification models which can highlight discriminative regions.
Although the trained model achieves good validation accuracy, it does not generalize well (82% test accuracy).
The re-trained model not only generalizes better (90% test accuracy), but also looks at the right regions (last column of Fig. ).
Fig. shows some examples of visual and textual explanations for the image classification model (VGG-16) trained on the Places365 dataset .
7) for the image classification model (VGG-16) trained on Places365 dataset ( ).
For a fully-convolutional architecture, CAM is a special case of Grad-CAM.
We propose a technique for producing 'visual explanations' for decisions from a large class of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based models, making them more transparent and explainable.
Second, when AI is on par with humans and reliably deployable (e.g., image classification trained on sufficient data), the goal is to establish appropriate trust and confidence in users.
Recently introduced deep residual networks (ResNets) are over 200-layers deep and have shown state-of-the-art performance in several challenging tasks.
As such, deep models are beginning to explore the spectrum between interpretability and accuracy.
In contrast, we make existing state-of-the-art deep models interpretable without altering their architecture, thus avoiding the interpretability vs. accuracy trade-off.
In contrast, we make existing state-of-the-art deep models interpretable without altering their architecture, thus avoiding the interpretability vs. accuracy trade-off.
In contrast, we make existing state-of-the-art deep models interpretable without altering their architecture, thus avoiding the interpretability vs. accuracy trade-off.

To summarize, our contributions are as follows: (1) We introduce Grad-CAM, a class-discriminative localization technique that generates visual explanations for any CNN-based network without requiring architectural changes or re-training.
5.3), where it outperforms baselines. (2) We apply Grad-CAM to existing top-performing classification, captioning (Sec.

For captioning and VQA, our visualizations expose that common CNN + LSTM models are often surprisingly good at localizing discriminative image regions despite not being trained on grounded image-text pairs. (3) We show a proof-of-concept of how interpretable Grad-CAM visualizations help in diagnosing failure modes by uncovering biases in datasets.

This is important not just for generalization, but also for fair and bias-free outcomes as more and more decisions are made by algorithms in society. (4) We present Grad-CAM visualizations for ResNets applied to image classification and VQA (Sec.
In future work, Selvaraju et al. proposed an approach to align gradient-based importances to human attention maps in order to ground vision and language models.
Unlike these, our approach achieves localization in one shot; it only requires a single forward and a partial backward pass per image and thus is typically an order of magnitude more efficient.
Models trained on biased datasets may not generalize to real-world scenarios, or worse, may perpetuate biases and stereotypes (w.r.t. gender, race, age, etc.).
Fig. shows some examples of visual and textual explanations for the image classification model (VGG-16) trained on the Places365 dataset .
